From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Flag of Brooklyn](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Brooklyn&redirect=no "Flag of Brooklyn"))

City of New York

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| [Flag of New York, NY (1977–present). Variant including the Latin inscription shown.](/wiki/File:Flag_of_New_York_City.svg) | |
| Proportion | 3:5 |
| Adopted | April 6, 1915 (modified December 30, 1977) |
| Design | A vertical tricolor of blue, white, and orange with a modified blue version of the [Seal of New York City](/wiki/Seal_of_New_York_City "Seal of New York City") in the center |
|  | |

The **flags of New York City** include the flag of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), the respective flags of the [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), and flags of certain city departments. The city flag is a vertical [tricolor](/wiki/Triband_(flag) "Triband (flag)") in [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue"), [white](/wiki/White "White"), and [orange](/wiki/Orange_(colour) "Orange (colour)") and charged in the center bar with the [seal of New York City](/wiki/Seal_of_New_York_City "Seal of New York City") in blue. The tricolor design is derived from the flag of the [Republic of the Seven United Netherlands](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic")—the [Prince's Flag](/wiki/Prince%27s_Flag "Prince's Flag")—as used in [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") in 1625, when that city became the capital of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Flag_of_New_York_City_%281914%E2%80%931915%29.png/250px-Flag_of_New_York_City_%281914%E2%80%931915%29.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_New_York_City_(1914%E2%80%931915).png)

Unofficial flag featuring the then-current city seal, used before the official flag of 1915

For the first few hundred years of its existence, the City of New York lacked an official flag.[[1]](#cite_note-pine-1) First mention of a city flag was by *[The New York herald](/wiki/The_New_York_Herald "The New York Herald")* in 1856.[[2]](#cite_note-2) During the Civil War the [10th New York Infantry Regiment](/wiki/10th_New_York_Infantry_Regiment "10th New York Infantry Regiment") was given a blue [regimental flag](/wiki/Military_colours,_standards_and_guidons "Military colours, standards and guidons") that bore the state seal in the front and the city's seal in the back.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) By the end of the 19th century, the city was flying an unofficial flag featuring its seal in blue on a white field.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1914, to commemorate the 250th anniversary of the installation of the first mayor under English rule, the City Art Commission appointed a blue-ribbon committee to create the city's first official seal and flag. The committee consulted with the [New-York Historical Society](/wiki/New-York_Historical_Society "New-York Historical Society") to study historical seals used by city government under the Dutch and English, to incorporate their symbolism into the new city seal and flag.[[1]](#cite_note-pine-1)[[6]](#cite_note-rampantbeavers-6)

The committee described their proposed flag this way:[[1]](#cite_note-pine-1)

> In our flag, the colors are Dutch, the arms are English, the crest is distinctively American, but the flag as such is the flag of the City, which has grown from these beginnings to be the home of all nations, the great cosmopolitan city of the world, the City of New York. — Committee of the Art Commission Associates, *Seal and Flag of the City of New York*, p. 84

The flag was approved on April 6, 1915, and first unveiled to the public on June 24.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/NYC_Flag.jpg/250px-NYC_Flag.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Flag.jpg)

The flag unfurled and fluttering, mounted on a city park yardarm

The current design dates from December 30, 1977, when the seal was subtly modified. The date was changed from 1664 (when the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") took possession) to 1625. The change was proposed by the Irish-born [Paul O'Dwyer](/wiki/Paul_O%27Dwyer "Paul O'Dwyer"), president of the City Council, to emphasize the Dutch contributions to the city's history and downplay the British legacy. The [choice of date](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_historical_anniversaries "List of New York City historical anniversaries") was controversial at the time; an aide to First Deputy Mayor James A. Cavanagh concluded: "In researching the validity of this proposal, I find no basis for 1625 as the founding date." An aide to then-mayor [Abe Beame](/wiki/Abe_Beame "Abe Beame") suggested that 1624 would be a more accurate date, as that was when the city was actually chartered as a legal Dutch entity. Author Edwin G. Burrows had another perspective on the debate, saying "You have to wonder if they didn't pick either 1626 or 1625 just to beat Boston, settled in 1630." Nonetheless, the mayor signed O'Dwyer's legislation.[[8]](#cite_note-birthdate-8)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Design")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/First_Flag_of_the_City_of_New_York%2C_1915.jpg/250px-First_Flag_of_the_City_of_New_York%2C_1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Flag_of_the_City_of_New_York,_1915.jpg)

The city's first official flag, 1915

Section 2-103 of the [New York City Administrative Code](/wiki/New_York_City_Administrative_Code "New York City Administrative Code") ("Official city flag") establishes the design as follows:

> A flag combining the colors orange, white and blue arranged in perpendicular bars of equal dimensions (the blue being nearest to the flagstaff) with the standard design of the seal of the city in blue upon the middle, or white bar, omitting the legend *Sigillum Civitatis Novi [Eboraci](/wiki/Eboracum "Eboracum")*, which colors shall be the same as those of the flag of the United Netherlands in use in the year sixteen hundred twenty-five.[[9]](#cite_note-code-9)

### Colors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/New_York_City_flag_1915.png/250px-New_York_City_flag_1915.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_flag_1915.png)

City's flag from 1915

The blue, white and orange refer to the colors of the historical flag of the Dutch monarchy. Though otherwise identical to the flag of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") ([Statenvlag](/wiki/Statenvlag "Statenvlag")), the Prince's Flag features an orange band in place of red. While New Amsterdam was under *de facto* control of the Dutch state, it was founded and operated by the [Dutch West Indies Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") (WIC). As a privately owned, yet royally [chartered company](/wiki/Chartered_company "Chartered company"), the WIC inherited the heraldic colors of the Dutch monarchy, orange representing the ancestral constituency of its founder, [William I, Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent"). The committee's report stated that "the order of arrangement follows the practice found in the [French](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France"), [Belgian](/wiki/Flag_of_Belgium "Flag of Belgium") and other tri-colors, of placing the darkest bar next to the staff."[[1]](#cite_note-pine-1)

The [New-York Historical Society](/wiki/New-York_Historical_Society "New-York Historical Society") originally proposed adopting a horizontal tricolor to be more reflective of the historical Dutch flags, but the committee kept the vertical orientation.[[6]](#cite_note-rampantbeavers-6)

### Symbolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Symbolism")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Flags_of_New_York_City "Special:EditPage/Flags of New York City") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(August 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

* **[Bald eagle](/wiki/Bald_eagle "Bald eagle")**: The symbol of the United States of America
* **Native American**: The original [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") inhabitants of the area
* **Seaman**: Symbolizes the European settlers of the area
* **Beaver**: Symbolizes the [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company"), which was the first company in New York (originally known as *[Nieuw Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam")*). Also the official animal of New York State.
* **Windmill**: Remembers the [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") history of the city and the prosperous industry of milling flour.
* **Flour barrels**: In the 17th century, New York had been granted a short-lived monopoly on milling, which established the fledgling colony as a commercial powerhouse
* **1625**: Originally 1664, the year was later changed to honor the establishment of New Amsterdam, which was actually settled in 1624.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The 1625 date has been described as "arbitrary" by the public historian at the New-York Historical Society and "simply wrong" by Michael Miscione, the Manhattan borough historian.[[8]](#cite_note-birthdate-8)

### Inconsistency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Inconsistency")]

Although the City Code states that the seal's Latin legend is to be omitted from the flag,[[9]](#cite_note-code-9) the city's own webpage shows a flag with the motto intact.[[11]](#cite_note-greenbook-11)

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Use")]

The flag is flown at [New York City Hall](/wiki/New_York_City_Hall "New York City Hall"), the [headquarters of the New York Police Department](/wiki/1_Police_Plaza "1 Police Plaza"), and at some city properties, such as public parks.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Bogart2006-13)

Several New York City sports teams have adopted the colors of the flag as their official team colors, including the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) and the [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer").[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2017, NYCFC added the tricolor city flag to its jersey, substituting its own "NYC" monogram in place of the seal.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") also use the orange, blue, and white color scheme; however, although the team moved to the New York City borough of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn,_New_York "Brooklyn, New York") in 2015, the team colors were selected when the team played in [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York"), [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), which also has blue and orange as official colors.[[18]](#cite_note-18) (They moved back to Long Island in 2020.)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Additionally, the [Syracuse Orange football](/wiki/Syracuse_Orange_football "Syracuse Orange football") jerseys follow an orange, blue and white color scheme; however, the Orange play one home game in the Bronx every few years, as the team usually plays at [JMA Wireless Dome](/wiki/JMA_Wireless_Dome "JMA Wireless Dome") in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York").

## Mayoral flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mayoral flag")]

* [![Standard of the mayor of New York City](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Flag_of_the_Mayor_of_New_York_City.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Mayor_of_New_York_City.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Mayor_of_New_York_City.svg "Standard of the mayor of New York City")

  Standard of the mayor of New York City

The office of the [mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City") has its own official variant, to which is added an arc of five five-pointed stars (representing each of the [five boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)")) in blue above the seal. The dimensions of the mayor's flag are set at 33 inches by 44 inches.[[11]](#cite_note-greenbook-11)

## Council flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Council flag")]

The [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") uses a variant of the city flag, with the word "Council" in capitals underneath the seal.[[11]](#cite_note-greenbook-11)

## Boroughs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Boroughs")]

Currently, only Brooklyn and the Bronx have official borough flags. The other three boroughs have standard designs in current use, though they have never been officially adopted. Staten Island borough lawmakers pushed to have their flag officially recognized by the state in the 1990s and early 2000s but were unsuccessful.[[20]](#cite_note-siflag-20)

### The Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Bronx")]

* [![Flag of The Bronx](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Flag_of_Borough_of_the_Bronx.svg/250px-Flag_of_Borough_of_the_Bronx.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Borough_of_the_Bronx.svg "Flag of The Bronx")

  Flag of The Bronx

The design of the flag of [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") consists of a horizontal tricolor. The top band is orange, the middle band is white, and the band at the bottom is blue, mimicking the historical Dutch tricolor. In the center of the flag is a laurel wreath denoting honor and fame. The wreath encircles the [Bronck family](/wiki/Jonas_Bronck "Jonas Bronck") arms. The shield of the family arms shows the face of the sun with rays displayed rising from the sea, signifying peace, liberty, and commerce. The crest of the arms is an eagle or an [auk](/wiki/Auk "Auk") facing eastward and with its wings expanded, representing "the hope of the New World while not forgetting the Old." The text underneath the shield is also the motto of the borough, and reads "ne cede malis", which is a Latin phrase meaning "Yield not to evil".[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-1913report-22)

The Bronx flag was first adopted in March 1912, by then-Borough President [Cyrus C. Miller](/wiki/Cyrus_C._Miller "Cyrus C. Miller"), and recognized by the city on June 29, 1915.[[23]](#cite_note-Aldermen1915-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Brooklyn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Brooklyn")]

* [![Flag of Brooklyn](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Flag_of_Brooklyn%2C_New_York.svg/250px-Flag_of_Brooklyn%2C_New_York.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Brooklyn,_New_York.svg "Flag of Brooklyn")

  Flag of Brooklyn

[![1895 engraving of Brooklyn Borough Hall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Brooklyn-Borough-Hall-1895.png/250px-Brooklyn-Borough-Hall-1895.png)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn-Borough-Hall-1895.png)

1895 engraving of [Brooklyn City Hall](/wiki/Brooklyn_Borough_Hall "Brooklyn Borough Hall"), showing Brooklyn's city flag on the right

The flag of Brooklyn has been in use since at least 1860 when Brooklyn was an independent city.[[25]](#cite_note-decoding-25)

The flag of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") has a white background with a seal at the center. Within the seal is an image of the Goddess of Justice set on a background of light blue, and bearing [fasces](/wiki/Fasces "Fasces"), a traditional emblem of unity. The fasces is composed of six rods, representing Brooklyn's original six towns (five Dutch, one English) under New Netherland.[[25]](#cite_note-decoding-25) Encircling that image is a ring of dark blue and the Old Dutch phrase "Een Draght Macht Maght" (modern Dutch: "Eendracht maakt macht") which translates into English as "[Unity makes strength](/wiki/Unity_makes_strength "Unity makes strength")". During the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") consolidation process of the 1890s, "Miss Brooklyn" from the flag became a popular figure, allegorized as being courted by [Father Knickerbocker](/wiki/Diedrich_Knickerbocker "Diedrich Knickerbocker"). Also in the darker ring are the words "Borough of Brooklyn". The outside and inside rim of the seal are gold-colored. The primary colors of the seal, blue and gold, have been adopted by the Borough President's office as Brooklyn's own colors. During [Grover Cleveland's inauguration](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") in 1885, members of the "Kings County Democracy" of Brooklyn carried a banner that bore a blue field with the city's seal in the center.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The flag was mentioned in the lyrics in a 1897 children's pledge, in which it was described as "...The city’s flag is white and blue"[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Manhattan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Manhattan")]

* [![Flag of the Borough of Manhattan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan.svg "Flag of the Borough of Manhattan")

  Flag of the Borough of Manhattan

The Borough of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")'s unofficial flag is very similar to the New York City flag. The only difference from the city flag is the use of the seal of the borough in place of the city seal. The seal is similar to the city's but circular in shape. It has two stars below and is encircled by the inscription "Borough of Manhattan November 1, 1683". The date at the bottom is the date on which the Province of New York was divided into twelve counties by New York Governor Thomas Dongan, and New York County (Manhattan) was created, with the same border in use today.

* [![Variant flag of Manhattan with simplified seal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan_%28variant%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan_%28variant%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Borough_of_Manhattan_(variant).svg "Variant flag of Manhattan with simplified seal")

  Variant flag of Manhattan with simplified seal

The borough has used variations on its official flag; featuring changes to the seal. One includes the encircling inscription "The President of the Borough of Manhattan NYC" to represent the institution in official occasions when the Borough President is present.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Another flag has been used by the borough, it consists of the same layout, with a simplified seal without the two stars removed from the seal, and with the date removed from the inscription.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Queens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Queens")]

* [![Flag of Queens](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Flag_of_Queens%2C_New_York.svg/250px-Flag_of_Queens%2C_New_York.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Queens,_New_York.svg "Flag of Queens")

  Flag of Queens

The flag of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") contains three horizontal bands, with the top and bottom being sky blue, and the middle white. These colors represent the arms of the first Dutch Governor [Willem Kieft](/wiki/Willem_Kieft "Willem Kieft"). At its center is a design consisting of a ring of [wampum](/wiki/Wampum "Wampum"), a tulip, and a rose. At the top-left of the flag is a crown, the words 'Queens Borough' stylized as 'Qveens Borovgh' emblazoned in gold, and 1898, the year the five boroughs were [consolidated](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1898%E2%80%931945) "History of New York City (1898–1945)").[[22]](#cite_note-1913report-22)

The symbols on the flag's design represent the borough's collective heritage with the [wampum](/wiki/Wampum "Wampum") paying homage to the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") natives who formerly called the land 'Seawanhaka' (a word meaning "island of sea shells") in reference to it as a place where they would collect clams and whelks used to make these beads. The tulip shown on the flag represents the Dutch, who were early settlers of the area. The red and white rose is a [Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose"), a traditional symbol of England and the English monarchy. The queen's crown signifies the namesake of the borough, which was named in honor of [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza"), Queen Consort of England in 1683, when New York's original twelve counties (of which Queens was one) were established.[[28]](#cite_note-queensflag-28)

The Queens flag was adopted on June 3, 1913, and first displayed four days later at a celebration marking the beginning of construction on the borough's dual rapid transit system.[[28]](#cite_note-queensflag-28)

### Staten Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Staten Island")]

* [![Flag of the Borough of Richmond, used until the borough was renamed "Staten Island" (1948–1975)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Ny-richmondborough.gif/250px-Ny-richmondborough.gif)](/wiki/File:Ny-richmondborough.gif "Flag of the Borough of Richmond, used until the borough was renamed \"Staten Island\" (1948–1975)")

  Flag of the Borough of Richmond, used until the borough was renamed "Staten Island" (1948–1975)

The current flag of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") was adopted in 2016.[[29]](#cite_note-citypurchases-29) The flag has flown over the [Staten Island Advance](/wiki/Staten_Island_Advance "Staten Island Advance") and Chamber of Commerce buildings, and is on display in City Hall and [Staten Island Borough Hall](/wiki/Staten_Island_Borough_Hall "Staten Island Borough Hall").

Staten Island has had three unofficial flags. The first was adopted at the same time [*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] as the flags of Brooklyn, Queens, and the Bronx. It featured a navy blue background with an orange seal in the center, containing two waterfowl and the text "Richmond Borough 1663 1898 New York". "Borough of Richmond" was the official name of Staten Island prior to 1975.

The next flag consisted of elements designed for a contest held in 1971 by Staten Island's Borough President [Robert T. Connor](/wiki/Robert_T._Connor "Robert T. Connor"). That flag had a white background with an oval in the center. Within the oval is a blue sky and two white seagulls. The green outline represented the countryside, and the white shape represented the [cityscape](/wiki/Cityscape "Cityscape"), denoting the residential areas of Staten Island. In the center of the oval were found the words "Staten Island" in gold. Under the name, five wavy blue lines symbolized the bodies of water surrounding the island.

The 1971 flag was criticized by Borough President [James Oddo](/wiki/James_Oddo "James Oddo"), who told the *[Staten Island Advance](/wiki/Staten_Island_Advance "Staten Island Advance")* that the current flag *"looks like the [Fresh Kills Landfill](/wiki/Fresh_Kills_Landfill "Fresh Kills Landfill"). The bird looks like a seagull, the mountain looks like a garbage pile."*[[20]](#cite_note-siflag-20)

In March 2016, Oddo created a new flag and new seal for the borough. Set in earth tones, each features an allegoric female figure representing the city standing on the island's shore and looking out onto the Narrows, where Henry Hudson's ship *The Half Moon* is at anchor. In the background is a small canoe with three oystermen; two native Staten Islanders, and the third a sailor from the *Half Moon*.[[25]](#cite_note-decoding-25) At the time, Oddo said that he had not decided if they would try to make the new flag official, like the flags of Brooklyn and the Bronx.[[20]](#cite_note-siflag-20) The City of New York purchased copies of this flag in 2017 for official functions.[[29]](#cite_note-citypurchases-29)

## City departments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: City departments")]

Several city departments have their own flags, for ceremonial or promotional use.

### Police Department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Police Department")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Police_Department.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Police_Department.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Police_Department.svg)

Flag of the New York City Police Department

The first Police Department flag was flown in 1861. It had the state's coat of arms with the word "Police" below.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The flag of the [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") that is used now was adopted in 1919.

It has twenty-four stars on a blue field, representing the cities, towns and villages that [consolidated in 1898](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") to form the present city. Five green and white stripes represent the five boroughs.

Officers killed in the line of duty have the police department flag draped over their caskets.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In 2023, the NYPD unveiled a new livery for NYPD patrol vehicles, incorporating design elements from the flag.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Fire Department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Fire Department")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/FDNYmemorialflag.png/250px-FDNYmemorialflag.png)](/wiki/File:FDNYmemorialflag.png)

Fire Department variant coffin flag

The flag of the [Fire Department of the City of New York](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") has five red and white stripes, representing the five boroughs. The canton features a [St. Florian's cross](/wiki/Christian_cross_variants#Saint's_crosses "Christian cross variants") with the city seal in the center, surrounded by a hook, ladder, fire hydrant and the letters "F.D.N.Y.".

The Fire Department uses a variant flag, in a vertical orientation with the [St. Florian's cross](/wiki/Christian_cross_variants#Saint's_crosses "Christian cross variants") turned on its side and gold fringe, draped over the caskets of fallen department members.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Department of Correction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Department of Correction")]

The flag of the [Department of Correction](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Correction "New York City Department of Correction") was adopted in 1998, upon the centennial of [the consolidation of New York City](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"). It features sixteen blue and white stripes, the same number of major facilities administered by the department at that time. On an orange canton sits the Seal of the City of New York in gold, surrounded by five stars for the five boroughs and the year "1895", when the department was created.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Parks and Recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Parks and Recreation")]

The flag of the [Parks Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "New York City Department of Parks and Recreation") features the department's leaf logo in green against a white field.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The Parks Department flag is flown on a [yardarm](/wiki/Yard_(sailing) "Yard (sailing)") over every park in New York City, alongside the city flag and beneath the flag of the United States of America.[[13]](#cite_note-Bogart2006-13) These yardarms were controversial when first introduced in 1997, partly because they were considered by some to be inappropriate outside of a nautical context and partly because it was seen by the former president of the city's Art Commission as over-reaching on the part of Parks Commissioner [Henry Stern](/wiki/Henry_Stern_(New_York_politician) "Henry Stern (New York politician)").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Sheriff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sheriff")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Sheriff%27s_Office.png/250px-Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Sheriff%27s_Office.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_New_York_City_Sheriff%27s_Office.png)

Flag of the New York City Sheriff's Office

The flag of the [New York City Sheriff's Office](/wiki/New_York_City_Sheriff%27s_Office "New York City Sheriff's Office") features a navy blue field, on which is the city's seal in blue against an oblong white background outlined in gold and red. The words "SHERIFF'S OFFICE" and "CITY OF NEW YORK" are set in gold horizontal text above and below the seal, respectively.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Sanitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flags_of_New_York_City&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sanitation")]

The [New York City Department of Sanitation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Sanitation "New York City Department of Sanitation") flag features the department's symbol – a [caduceus](/wiki/Caduceus "Caduceus") with the letter "S" superimposed upon it – against a blue field, surrounded by the words "THE CITY OF NEW YORK" and "DEPARTMENT OF SANITATION" in gold.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
